response:
A haiku is a traditional Japanese poem with three lines and a 5-7-5 syllable structure. I need to write one about programming.

Let me think of programming concepts: code, bugs, debugging, compiling, algorithms, logic, syntax errors, runtime, etc.

Possible haiku:

Syntax error found
Searching through lines of logic
A semicolon

Let me count syllables:
Line 1: Syn-tax er-ror found = 5 syllables
Line 2: Search-ing through lines of log-ic = 6? Let me recount: Search-ing (2) through (1) lines (1) of (1) log-ic (2) = 7? Actually: Search-ing (2) through (1) lines (1) of (1) log-ic (2) = 2+1+1+1+2 = 7. Yes.
Line 3: A sem-i-co-lon = 5 syllables? A (1) sem-i (2) co (1) lon (1) = 5. Yes.

That works. But let me see if I can make it more evocative.

Another idea:

Endless loops of thought
Debugging the silent crash
A single fix works

Count: End-less loops of thought (5) - End-less (2) loops (1) of (1) thought (1) = 5. Debug-ging the si-lent crash (7) - De-bug-ging (3) the (1) si-lent (2) crash (1) = 7. A sin-gle fix works (5) - A (1) sin-gle (2) fix (1) works (1) = 5. Good.

I'll go with the first one as it's classicSyntax error found—
searching through lines of logic.
A semicolon.
